title: Mid-turn interrupt: Ctrl+C cancels the in-flight turn, not the process
current_state: KeyboardInterrupt during a long tool loop kills the whole REPL (run() catches it only between turns and exits). A runaway 10-tool chain cannot be stopped without losing the session.
target_state: Ctrl+C during a turn aborts the current tool loop at the next safe boundary (between tool calls / between LLM rounds), records the partial turn with an 'interrupted' marker in self.messages (so the conversation stays API-valid: pending tool_use gets a synthetic 'cancelled by user' tool_result), and returns to the prompt. Ctrl+C at the idle prompt keeps today's exit-with-flush behavior.
migration_plan: 1. Wrap the tool loop body in try/except KeyboardInterrupt; on catch, append synthetic tool_results for any unanswered tool_use ids ('[cancelled by user]'), append a brief assistant text turn, break.
2. The message-pairing invariant (no orphan tool_use) is the critical part — extend truncation safe-slice tests to cover the interrupted shape.
3. Distinguish idle-prompt Ctrl+C (current behavior) from in-turn Ctrl+C.
4. Tests: simulate KeyboardInterrupt from a tool handler; assert messages list stays valid for the next router.chat call and turn is logged with error='interrupted'.
risk_notes: pi_agent.py is RISK_FLAGGED → interactive implementation. The orphan-tool_use invariant is exactly the write/read-divergence bug class — test it hard.
